Title: Knocking out the carboxyltransferase interactor 1 (CTI1) in Chlamydomonas boosted oil content by fivefold without affecting cell growth

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Carboxyltransferase interactor 1; chloroplast transit peptide; single transmembrane domain; coiled-coil domain
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carbon and energy storage; biotechnology feedstock oil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Knockout of CrCTI1 increases total fatty acids by >25% and TAG by up to fivefold to sevenfold depending on condition; under heterotrophic and ambient CO2 conditions mutants show growth defects despite elevated oil
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of three independent alleles; RT-PCR validation; GC-MS fatty acid profiling; TLC-based TAG quantification; BODIPY/confocal lipid droplet imaging; growth assays; proteomics; Y2H interaction assay with Cra-CT
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reported to increase under very low CO2 conditions (cited from Zheng et al., 2014)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mixotrophic growth with acetate; photoautotrophic growth under ambient air or 2% CO2; heterotrophic growth in dark with acetate; nitrogen starvation
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cra-CT; CAS1 (co-immunoprecipitation reported previously, cited background)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrCTI1 is the single CTI homolog in Chlamydomonas and physically interacts with the ACCase alpha-carboxyltransferase subunit Cra-CT. 2. CRISPR knockout mutants accumulate substantially more total fatty acids and TAG, with clear lipid droplet overaccumulation. 3. CrCTI1 therefore functions as a negative regulator of de novo fatty acid synthesis and carbon partitioning, especially under carbon-limited conditions. 4. Loss of CrCTI1 alters proteomes linked to carbon metabolism, beta-oxidation, and antioxidant defense.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Growth repression and eventual loss of viability during prolonged heterotrophic growth in the dark; slower growth under ambient CO2 photoautotrophic conditions; elevated ROS under ambient CO2
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising engineering target to boost algal oil production; knockout or switchable repression could be deployed in two-step cultivation strategies for industrial lipid production, though carbon-limitation-associated growth penalties must be managed
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although CrCTI1 functions as a regulator of fatty acid synthesis through interaction with ACCase, it is not a transcription factor and is best classified here as a Common Gene. Formerly named CPP1.
